coredata.dc:title: Nanoarchitectonics of Melia Dubia flowers to fluorescent carbon dots and its Ferritin sensing
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Kathiravan, Arunkumar
Premkumar, Suguna
Jhonsi, Mariadoss Asha
coredata.dc:description: In this study, the Melia Dubia flowers was utilized as a precursor for the synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanomaterial by hydrothermal method. As synthesized carbon nanomaterial have been extensively characterized and demonstrated notable characteristics, including a particle size ranging from 10 to 20 nm and excitation dependent fluorescence. Intriguingly, the carbon nanomaterial exhibited remarkable sensitivity, as demonstrated by detection limit of 23 nM for Fe3+ ions. By taking advantage of time-resolved fluorescence quenching experiments, we have satisfactorily elucidated the sensing process. The observation of a decrease in fluorescence decay in the presence of Fe3+ provides direct evidence and substantiates the occurrence of an electron transfer quenching mechanism. In addition, the selectivity of CDs has been used to detect Fe3+ in ferritin (non-heme metalloprotein). The current investigation demonstrates that Melia Dubia flowers can be utilized to develop inexpensive CDs that could serve as a fluorescent probe for the sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ ions and Ferritin.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Melia Dubia flowers
Carbon dots
Fe3+ ions
Sensor
LOD

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: A.K. would like to express gratitude to Vel Tech Rangarajan Dr. Sagunthala R & D Institute of Science and Technology for funding the analytical characterization charges. A.K. also thanks the sophisticated analytical instruments facility (SAIF), Indian Institute of Technology, Chennai, for the fluorescence lifetime measurements. MAJ thanks the BSACIST for providing the seed grant (Ref. No. CSD/CSM/2022/08, dated: 14/07/2022) for conducting independent research.

A.K. would like to express gratitude to Vel Tech Rangarajan Dr. Sagunthala R & D Institute of Science and Technology for funding the analytical characterization charges. A.K. also thanks the sophisticated analytical instruments facility (SAIF), Indian Institute of Technology, Chennai, for the fluorescence lifetime measurements. MAJ thanks the BSACIST for providing the seed grant (Ref. No. CSD/CSM/2022/08, dated: 14/07/2022) for conducting independent research.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM, (b) HR-TEM at 50 nm scale bar and (c) Raman spectrum of derived CDs. Histogram plot of HRTEM image (inset of Fig. 1b).

(a) UV-Visible absorption spectrum (inset: digital image of the CDs under UV light), (b) Excitation dependent fluorescence spectra of CDs and (c) fluorescence decay of CDs at the excitation of 390 nm.
Effect of pH (a), storage time (b) and ionic strength (c) on the fluorescence intensity of CDs.

Detection of Fe3+ using CDs (a) selectivity, (b) effect of Fe3+ (0.01–3 μM) in fluorescence spectra of CDs and (c) plot between change fluorescence intensity vs Fe3+ concentrations for LOD determination.
(a) Stern-Volmer plot and (b) time-resolved fluorescence decay for the fluorescence quenching of CDs by Fe3+ ions.
A plot of change in the fluorescence intensity of CDs with different amounts of ferritin at pH 6.

head.ce:title: Nanoarchitectonics of Melia Dubia flowers to fluorescent carbon dots and its Ferritin sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry & Centre for Molecular Photoscience, Vel Tech Rangarajan Dr. Sagunthala R & D Institute of Science and Technology, Avadi, Chennai 600062, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry & Centre for Molecular Photoscience, Vel Tech Rangarajan Dr. Sagunthala R & D Institute of Science and Technology, Avadi

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry & Centre for Molecular Photoscience, Vel Tech Rangarajan Dr. Sagunthala R & D Institute of Science and Technology, Avadi, Chennai - 600 062, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, B. S. Abdur Rahman Crescent Institute of Science and Technology, Chennai 600048, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, B. S. Abdur Rahman Crescent Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, B. S. Abdur Rahman Crescent Institute of Science and Technology, Chennai-600 048, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, the Melia Dubia flowers was utilized as a precursor for the synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanomaterial by hydrothermal method. As synthesized carbon nanomaterial have been extensively characterized and demonstrated notable characteristics, including a particle size ranging from 10 to 20 nm and excitation dependent fluorescence. Intriguingly, the carbon nanomaterial exhibited remarkable sensitivity, as demonstrated by detection limit of 23 nM for Fe3+ ions. By taking advantage of time-resolved fluorescence quenching experiments, we have satisfactorily elucidated the sensing process. The observation of a decrease in fluorescence decay in the presence of Fe3+ provides direct evidence and substantiates the occurrence of an electron transfer quenching mechanism. In addition, the selectivity of CDs has been used to detect Fe3+ in ferritin (non-heme metalloprotein). The current investigation demonstrates that Melia Dubia flowers can be utilized to develop inexpensive CDs that could serve as a fluorescent probe for the sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ ions and Ferritin.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Development and deployment of various heavy metal ions sensors have gained much interest among the research community owing to their significant role in human life as well as environment protection [1]. In recent decades, the rise in various industrial activities of human beings leads the heavy metal ions toxicity in natural resources [2]. This issue must be looked at as a serious threat to life especially on land and water. Iron is one of the heavy metals which has gained importance in detection and monitoring of its level in an ecosystem as well as human body. It’s a necessary element present in the human body and plants which play a vital role in different metabolic activities such as enzymatic catalysis, electron transfer and as an oxygen carrier inside a species metabolism[3]. So, it is important to maintain the level of iron for the proper functioning of the physiological process as it may cause either anemia or Alzheimer’s diseases with respect to its low or high levels respectively[4]. Moreover, WHO and EPA has given the limit of iron in portable water which is 2000 µg /L [5]. Based on this context, the researchers are interested in detecting the level of iron in various natural resources as well as living species [6,7]. From the literature it is observed that number of methods are available for the sensing of iron both qualitative and quantitative analysis, which includes spectrometry, voltammetry, chromatography, colorimetric and electro thermal methods [8–11]. On the other hand, numerous materials were reported to be used as a probe to detect iron such as nanomaterials, chelating ligands, charge transfer complexes and recently carbon-based materials [12–14]. Ultimately these chemosensors should be feasible, reliable, economical, long lasting, and accurate etc. [15]. Furthermore, the mechanism of sensing via various methods has also been discussed in the literature such as complexation, electron transfer, energy transfer, inner filter effect, and so on [16–18]. It should be noted that each method of detection has its own pros and cons specifically in terms of high-end machinery requirement, need for skilled professionals, environmental unfriendliness, optimization to conduct the testing in ambient conditions, high cost and non-facile operation techniques etc. [19–21]. Among the various materials and methods used so far towards the detection of iron, fluorescence-based sensors stand out with its feasibility, simplicity, precision, proper mechanism, onsite utilization etc. [22]. It is believed that fluorescence-based sensors are one of the best analytical tools for heavy metal ions detection [22]. Regarding the probe for sensing in recent years, fluorescent carbon dots are widely used in chemo and biosensing of various analytes with high precision owing to its unique properties such as high-water solubility, stability, non-toxic, biocompatibility, functionality, surface properties, rich in fluorescence quantum yield etc. [23]. There are two distinct methodologies for the development of these CDs, namely the top-down and bottom-up approaches. A top-down approach converts the bulk carbon precursors such as diamond, carbon black, graphite, ash, and other macroscopic carbon materials into CDs via various techniques such as laser ablation, electrochemical, and direct synthesis of CDs. In the bottom-up methodology, a small organic molecule undergoes conversion into CDs using various techniques such as hydrothermal treatment, microwave irradiation, pyrolysis, and others. The hydrothermal method is commonly favoured for the synthesis of CDs owing to its distinctive characteristics, including simplicity, environmental sustainability, cost-effectiveness, and scalability [24]. These fluorescence CDs contains sp2 hybridized carbon core with sp3 carbon insertions at the edge and surrounded by various functional moieties such as hydroxyl, epoxy, carbonyl, carboxyl, and amine with respect to the elements originated from its precursors [25]. And so, the selection of precursors plays an vital role in tweaking the properties of these CDs. Thus, owing to many supplementary properties, natural sources are widely used as precursors in the recent past and have been documented for the synthesis of CDs. These sources include coffee beans, tomato juice, lemon juice, ginkgo leaves, tamarind, Lantana camara berries and tulsi leaves [26–30]. A detailed review on natural sources derived CDs is also available in the literature [31,32]. Significant advantages like the affordability and lack of toxicity of flowers make them one of the most exploited natural resources owing to their rich content of flavonoids, triterpenoids, phenolic acids, and fatty acid esters.

In the present work, we have developed a novel fluorescent based chemosensor to detect iron which comprises carbon dots derived from Melia Dubia flowers (commonly known as Malai Vembu) via hydrothermal bottom-up approach. By use of this method, high quality CDs can be produced on a large scale without the assistance of any chemical reagent. The main chemical components of M. Dubia flower include flavonoids, saponins, fatty acids, proteins, alkaloids, and polysaccharides, which contribute to great medicinal and nutritional values, and it also contains many active materials that contribute to not only pharmaceutical effects but also chemical reactions, Moreover, flavonoids in flowers have high affinity towards metal ion such as Fe3+ ion. Hence, flavonoid rich M. Dubia acts as green precursors in the synthesis of the carbon dots. So far, no CDs developed from M. Dubia flowers have been reported. Thus, derived fluorescent carbon dots are effectively used as a probe to sense Fe3+ ion via spectrofluorimetric analysis.

The required starting materials and reagents for this work were procured from commercial sources (Sigma-Aldrich and Avra chemicals, India). Several metal ions (Mn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, CO2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Sn2+, Cd2+, Hg2+ and Pb2+) were procured from TCI Chemicals, the precursor Melia Dubia flowers is collected from the BSACIST institute campus. Float-A-Lyzer dialysis membrane is purchased from H-media laboratories. Shimadzu UV-1800 spectrophotometer was used to record the UV-Visible absorption spectra. The photophysical and sensing experiments were carried out with the help of a Hitachi F-4500 fluorescence spectrometer. To quantify the fluorescence lifetime of CDs, the Fluorocube - Lifetime System (JOBIN-VYON) was used. To investigate the morphology of the CDs, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was performed with a Nova NanoSEM 600 from FEI, Netherlands, and EDS analysis was performed with an EDAX system. A Philips Tecnai F20 G2 FEI-TEM was used to measure high resolution transmission electron microscopic (HR-TEM). UV–visible absorption, steady-state fluorescence, time-resolved fluorescence, and sensing measurements were all performed using high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) grade solvents.

Most of the biomass derived carbonaceous nanomaterials were followed by hydrothermal methodology owing to its facile, economical and one pot facilities. So, we have also chosen the same for the development of CDs using Melia Dubia flowers as precursor. The detailed synthetic methodology is as follows. As a first step, the freshly collected Melia Dubia flowers are washed with distilled water by soaking them for 10 min in order to remove the dirt and other unwanted materials. Followed by it, the flower contents are filtered to drain water and kept it for dry for some time, then around 10 g of the cleaned flower mass is ground into paste using a normal kitchen blender and mixed with approx. 100 ml of distilled water under stirring for 15 min. At this stage it is observed that the mixture is in pale purple in color. Second step, the mixture of flower paste with water is transferred to a 200 ml capacity of Teflon autoclave which is coated with stainless steel and tightly closed with its lids through nuts and bolts. Further the autoclave is kept in a furnace at the temperature of 180 °C for the period of 8 h. Once the reaction time is completed the autoclave is kept it cool to the room temperature naturally and it is observed that the change of purple biomass into dark brown crude which confirms the conversion of Melia Dubia flowers into carbonaceous nanomaterial (CDs). This is in accordance with our previous reports as well as from the literature. The third stage of the synthesis is separation and purification of CDs from the dark brown liquid with biomass crude. The separation process which involves, filtration through normal filter paper followed by centrifugation of the crude. This step is done to remove the unreacted biomass and the clear yellow solution is washed with dichloromethane 3 times to remove the unwanted organic impurities present in the reaction mixture. Further the resulting mixture is filtered through Whatmann filter paper to get rid of suspended particles and filtered via 0.2 µm cellulose filter paper using syringe filtration method. This will yield the carbon nanomaterials in the maximum size range of 200 nm which is processed for volume reduction of excess solvent (water) from the reaction mixture through condensation procedure. As a last step of the synthesis the dark brown colored mixed size nanomaterial (1–200 nm) derived from Melia Dubia flowers is kept for dialysis using membrane tubes with MWCO of 1000 kDa in water medium for the period of 24 h. In order to avoid the deposition of nanoparticles on the side walls of membrane tube and for the effective dialysis process, the dialysis water was changed for every 6 h of duration. Finally, the derived CDs from the dialysis tube which is free from other contamination and in the size range of nano is stored at 15 °C for future use and characterization. From the above methodology we could be able to derive carbonaceous nanomaterials with high purity and high product yield (40 %), no further functionalization process is required since the derived material itself contains various functional moieties on its surface.

The fluorescence quantum yield (ϕ F) measurements of CDs in water were conducted and the value of ϕ F is calculated using the following Eq. (1).
(1)
ϕ F/ϕ R = (I S/I R) × (F R/F S) × (η S/η R)2 →

where, the S and R refers to the sample (CDs) and the reference (quinine sulphate, ϕ R= 0.54), respectively. F is the fraction of light absorbed (1–10−A) at the excitation wavelength (366 nm), I is the integrated area of the fluorescence spectrum, and η is refractive index of the solvent.

To investigate the selectivity of CDs, each metal ion (1 μM) solution was added to a set of CDs solutions individually. The fluorescence spectrum of each sample solution was then monitored at 370 nm excitation wavelength to determine the Fe3+ ion selectivity. To determine the sensitivity, the fluorescence titration method was used. The Fe3+ ion solution (up to 30 μL; the maximum concentration of Fe3+ ion in solution is 20 μM) was gradually added into the CDs solution in a quartz cuvette in a typical assay. Prior to recording the fluorescence spectrum, the solution was thoroughly mixed. The sensitivity of Fe3+ ion was assessed using changes in the fluorescence intensity of CDs after Fe3+ ion addition.

The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated from the fluorescence quenching data by using equation 3 σ/K, where σ is the standard deviation of the fluorescence intensity, and K is the slope of the linear plot.

The developed fluorescent CDs probe was used to detect Fe3+ ions in-situ real water samples (pond, lake and tap water). Water samples were purified by filtration through a 0.22 µm filter membrane (Millipore) and centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 30 min. In each water sample, a CDs stock solution was prepared, and the absorbance was set to 0.3. Various concentrations of Fe3+ ion solution was added to each set of CDs solution using the standard addition method. Owing to the rapid response nature of CDs, no incubation time was maintained for recovery studies. The fluorescence intensity of CDs is not altered in all water samples, indicating that there are no intrinsic Fe3+ ions in the collected environmental water samples. In addition, the standard colorimetric thiocyanate method has been adapted to test for the content of Fe3+ in real water samples. Intriguingly, comparable results were obtained, revealing that the analyzed water samples do not contain intrinsic Fe3+ ions. When different concentrations (0.5, 1, 3 and 5 μM) of Fe3+ ions were spiked into the water samples, the fluorescence intensity of CDs is altered and able to measure the Fe3+ concentrations.

The conversion of luminescent Melia Dubia flowers derived carbonaceous nanomaterial (CDs) via hydrothermal methodology is authenticated by the initial transformation of the precursor solution’s color from purple to dark brown once the reaction has completed. Surface morphology and elemental composition of the derived CDs were analyzed by SEM and HRTEM ( Fig. 1). It is observed that particles are spherical and uniformly scattered on the surface which was visualized in Fig. 1a. The HRTEM image of CDs (Fig. 1b) captured in 50 nm scale clearly shows that the derived CDs are quasi spherical in shape with the uniform particles of size ranges from 10 to 20 nm (histogram plot shown in inset of Fig. 1b). The Raman spectral analysis of CDs measured at 532 nm excitation wavelength is shown in Fig. 1c. The spectrum clearly reveals that two distinct peaks at 1379 cm−1 with the lower peak intensity and the other one at 1589 cm−1 with higher peak intensity. The former peak is corresponding to sp2 hybridized carbon atoms (G band) and the later one is corresponding to sp3 hybridized carbon atoms (D band). The D and G bands of carbonaceous nanomaterial representing the amount of defect and graphitization respectively associated with the prepared CDs. The peak intensity ratio of G band to the D band is observed to be 1.8. The high intensity ratio makes it evident that the more graphitized carbon structure formation in CDs. The FTIR spectrum of CDs is recorded to identify its surface functionalities (Fig. S1). A broad peak from 3700 to 2900 cm–1 is attributed to -OH and N-H stretching vibrations. The peaks at 1675 cm−1, 1400 cm−1 and 1060 cm−1 are assigned to stretching vibrations of CO, CC, and C-O-C, respectively. This figure clearly indicates the presence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups.

Photophysical studies of the prepared CDs were done using UV–visible absorption, fluorescence and time-resolved fluorescence techniques ( Fig. 2). There are two shapeless bands observed in the absorption spectrum around 280 nm and 340 nm, respectively. These two bands are originated from π − π * transition from the CC π-conjugated sp2 hybridization and the other one is from n − π * transition from the functional groups attached with carbon core, respectively (Fig. 2a). The derived CDs exhibit blue emission as shown in the digital image of the aqueous dispersed CDs under UV light (inset of Fig. 2a). It is observed from the fluorescence spectral analysis, CDs is exploring the excitation dependent fluorescence behavior which is the typical characteristic of carbonaceous nanomaterial. From the Fig. 2b, it is evident that while varying the excitation wavelength from 280 to 460 nm at 20 nm intervals, the emission behavior of CDs is varying such as the emission intensity is increased slowly reached a maximum at the excitation of 340 nm followed by declined as well as red shifted till 460 nm of excitation. This type of excitation dependent emission may due to the presence of various functional moieties on the surface of the derived CDs. Further the fluorescence quantum yield of the prepared CDs has also been analyzed in comparison with the quinine sulphate standard and the calculated quantum yield is 0.59 %. The value of quantum yield is suitable enough for the fluorescence-based sensing applications and it is comparable with the fluorescent quantum yield of other natural biomass derived carbonaceous nanomaterials. Moreover, the excited state lifetime of the derived CDs has also been measured using time correlated single photon counting technique. At the excitation of 390 nm, the CDs yields a bi-exponential decay curve (Fig. 2c). The obtained fluorescence decay curve is fitted with a bi-exponential function A 1exp(-t/τ 1) + A 2 exp(-t/τ 2), and the average fluorescence decay lifetime (τ av) of 4.15 ns is defined in the usual way as τ av = (A 1 τ 1 + A 2 τ 2)/ (A 1 + A 2). Thus, the prepared CDs is emissive in nature and suitable as fluorimetric sensor for specific analytes.

To investigate the stability of CDs, effect of several variables was examined including pH, storage time and ionic strength. Fig. 3a shows that the CDs have a steady fluorescence intensity at a pH interval of 3–11, but it drops at higher pH (> 11) and lower pH (<3), which can be attributed to chemical interactions of surface functional groups via strong intermolecular hydrogen bonding under extremely acidic and basic pH conditions. As a result of the pH findings, CDs are great candidates for anions or cations detection. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of CDs does not drop significantly during storage for up to three months (Fig. 3b). In addition, no discernible reduction in fluorescence intensity was detected at NaCl concentrations up to 1 M (Fig. 3c). All of these characteristics strongly endorse the fact that as-obtained CDs exhibit stable fluorescence features, which have considerable potential for practical applications such as ion detection under challenging environments.

The inherent fluorescence stability of CDs is utilized for the purpose of detecting metal ions in an aqueous medium. The present study aimed to assess the detection capabilities of the CDs for the sensing of Fe3+ ions in water. In order to investigate the specificity of detection by the CDs, a range of metal ions were utilized to assess their impact on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs when subjected to excitation by 340 nm. The bar chart depicted in Fig. 4a provides a comparative analysis of quenching efficiency of CDs subsequent to the introduction of various metal ions (3 µM). As shown in Fig. 4a, the fluorescence of CDs is noticeably quenched by Fe3+ ions, while other metal ions exhibited a relatively minor effect. The results of this study indicate that the CDs have significant potential for the specific sensor probe of Fe3+ in aqueous solution. In addition, the interference effect of various metal ions on CDs selectivity towards Fe3+ was investigated. The fluorescence spectra of CDs with Fe3+ (3 µM) in the presence of other metal ions (3 µM) were investigated. Fig. S2 shows that the quenching efficiency of CDs/Fe3+ was not significantly affected by other metal ions. The investigation demonstrates that other metal ions have no impact on Fe3+ sensing. In order to ascertain the sensitivity of CDs, an investigation was conducted to analyze the fluorescence intensity by introducing varied concentrations of Fe3+ ranging from 0.01 μM to 3 μM. According to the findings presented in Fig. 4b, it is observed that the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots (CDs) diminishes as the concentration of Fe3+ increases. Fig. 4c illustrates the relationship between the I0/I ratio and the concentrations of Fe3+. An excellent linear fitting was observed within the concentration range of 0.01–3 μM. The limit of detection (LOD) was determined to be 23 nM using the 3σ/s principle, where σ represents the standard deviation of 10 blank tests and s is the slope of the linear plot. In addition, the standard colorimetric thiocyanate method has been adapted to test for the content of Fe3+ in water samples. Intriguingly, comparable results were obtained. The observed value falls below the established safety threshold for Fe3+ concentration in drinking water (0.3 mg/L or 5.4 μM), indicating that CDs are capable of meeting the criteria for evaluating Fe3+ in a water-based environment. Intriguingly, when examining table S1, it becomes evident that the CDs derived from Melia Dubia flowers waste have superior Fe3+ sensing capabilities in comparison to other CDs [33–37]. This is due to their advantageous characteristics, such as the excellent selectivity and sensitivity, a relatively wide detection range, and ease of material synthesis.

The next phase of investigation centers on elucidating the quenching process responsible for the steady decrease in fluorescence intensity of CDs induced by the presence of Fe3+ ions. To understand whether the observed quenching is due to either complexation or a static process, we have conducted UV–visible absorption spectral measurements of CDs with varying Fe3+ ion concentrations. Fig. S3 depicts the changes in the absorption spectrum of the CDs with respect to Fe3+ concentration. No new peak formation or spectral shift has been observed, which clearly indicates that the absence of ground state complex formation occurred between the CDs and Fe3+ ions. These results clearly show that the quenching is not due to a static process. Further, the examination of the quenching data has been carried out using the broadly accepted Stern-Volmer equation (I0/I = 1 + KSV[Q]), which enables an understanding of the quenching mechanism. where I0 and I represent the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively, and [Q] represents the concentration of Fe3+. As depicted in Fig. 5a, the fluorescence quenching data are in agreement with the Stern-Volmer equation. The linear Stern-Volmer plot provides direct evidence that the observed quenching phenomenon is a result of dynamic processes, specifically either electron or energy transfer. The emission spectra of CDs and absorption spectra of Fe3+ do not exhibit significant overlap within the wavelength range of 350–600 nm. This suggests that the energy transfer mechanism from the excited CDs to Fe3+ can be disregarded. In order to validate the electron transfer quenching process, we conducted an analysis of the fluorescence decay data obtained from excitation at a wavelength of 390 nm. Fig. 5b displays the time-resolved fluorescence decays of the CDs, both in the absence and presence of Fe3+ ions. As already stated, the fluorescence decay of the CDs was analyzed using a bi-exponential function with the lifetimes of τ1 = 2.05 ns (62 %) and τ2 = 7.58 ns (38 %), resulting in an average lifetime of 4.15 ns. When Fe3+ ions are introduced, the fluorescence decay of the CDs is shortened with the lifetimes of τ1 = 1.11 ns (69 %) and τ2 = 3.62 ns (31 %). The decreased fluorescence lifetime (τ av = 1.88 ns) provided direct evidence that the quenching of the excited state is a result of electron transfer from the CDs to the partially occupied 3d orbital of the Fe3+ ions. Consequently, it is our contention that the primary mechanisms responsible for the overall quenching phenomenon are the oxidative electron transfer events occurring between CDs and Fe3+ ions.

Considering the unique properties of CDs, such as their extreme affinity, selectivity, and sensitivity to Fe3+ ions, our investigation extended to examine the interactions between CDs and ferritin. Since, ferritin serves as the principal protein responsible for storing iron and is crucial for the maintenance of iron homeostasis [38]. A lack of ferritin inside the human body can give rise to the condition of anemia, while an excessive amount of ferritin might potentially cause iron overload sickness and hemochromatosis [39]. As a result, ferritin identification and evaluation are critical in the clinical sector. The CDs that were obtained from Melia Dubia flowers exhibited emission quenching when exposed to ferritin at a concentration of 60 μg ml−1. The suppression of emission could potentially be attributed to the higher amount of Fe3+ within ferritin [40]. The fluorescence titration experiments conducted on CDs with ferritin demonstrated a linear decrease in fluorescence intensity as the concentration of ferritin increased within the range of 0–3 μg ml−1. The LOD was determined to be 0.25 μg ml−1 based on the linear relationship observed in the plot ( Fig. 6). This LOD value is consistent with previously reported values for the detection of ferritin using fluorescence-based methods [41–43].

In a nutshell, the fluorescent CDs were successfully synthesized utilizing Melia Dubia flowers through a one-step hydrothermal method without adding any special additives. The CDs demonstrate remarkable fluorescence stability within the pH range of 5–9, and they exhibit notable endurance against ionic strength. The CDs have a prominent ability to detect Fe3+ ions with excellent sensitivity and selectivity. This detection is achieved through a fluorescence quenching method. Intriguingly, the linear detection ranges from 0.01 to 3 μM, with a LOD of 23 nM was attained. Thus, the present work demonstrates that the utilization of Melia Dubia flowers is advantageous in the development of environmentally friendly and cost-effective fluorescent carbon nanomaterial. In addition, the CDs were used to detect ferritin albumin, with a sensitivity of 0.25 μg ml−1. Overall, the developed carbon material will be a prospective fluorescent probe for the sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ in an aqueous condition.

Arunkumar Kathiravan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Mariadoss Asha Jhonsi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Suguna Premkumar: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper. Not applicable.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: A.K. would like to express gratitude to Vel Tech Rangarajan Dr. Sagunthala R & D Institute of Science and Technology for funding the analytical characterization charges. A.K. also thanks the sophisticated analytical instruments facility (SAIF), Indian Institute of Technology, Chennai, for the fluorescence lifetime measurements. MAJ thanks the BSACIST for providing the seed grant (Ref. No. CSD/CSM/2022/08, dated: 14/07/2022) for conducting independent research.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2023.132824.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Optical detection of Fe3+ ions in aqueous solution with high selectivity and sensitivity by using sulfasalazine functionalized microgels
Sensors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Ji, Z. Zhu, S. Dong, J. Nie, B. Du, Optical Detection of Fe3+ Ions in Aqueous Solution with High Selectivity and Sensitivity by Using Sulfasalazine Functionalized Microgels, Sensors 19 (2019) 4223. https://doi.org/10.3390/S19194223.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescence “turn-off” sensing of iron (III) ions utilizing pyrazoline based sensor: experimental and computational study
J. Fluor.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Sharma, S. Bhogal, I. Mohiuddin, M. Yusuf, A.K. Malik, Fluorescence “Turn-off” Sensing of Iron (III) Ions Utilizing Pyrazoline Based Sensor: Experimental and Computational Study, J Fluoresc. 32 (2022) 2319–2331. https://doi.org/10.1007/S10895–022-03024-Y/METRICS.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Iron catalysis of lipid peroxidation in ferroptosis: regulated enzymatic or random free radical reaction?
Free Radic. Biol. Med.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.A. Stoyanovsky, Y.Y. Tyurina, I. Shrivastava, I. Bahar, V.A. Tyurin, O. Protchenko, S. Jadhav, S.B. Bolevich, A.V. Kozlov, Y.A. Vladimirov, A.A. Shvedova, C.C. Philpott, H. Bayir, V.E. Kagan, Iron catalysis of lipid peroxidation in ferroptosis: Regulated enzymatic or random free radical reaction?, Free Radic Biol Med. 133 (2019) 153–161. https://doi.org/10.1016/J.FREERADBIOMED.2018.09.008.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A histochemical study of iron, transferrin, and ferritin in Alzheimer’s diseased brains
J. Neurosci. Res.
